name: Liu Bei (Xuande)
page_title: Liu Bei (Xuande) 劉備 (玄德) [Han, Gongsun Zan, Tao Qian, Wei, Yuan Shao, Liu Biao, Shu] – Romance of the Three Kingdoms Encyclopedia – Kongming’s Archives
novel_notes:
Differences Between Fact and Common Fiction
	
		
	The Peach Tree Garden oath did not actually take place but Sanguozhi states that Liu, Guan and Zhang ‘slept in the same bed and were as close as brothers.’
	Played a minor role during the Yellow Scarves revolt. Did not fight the Scarves at Wan or save Dong Zhuo. Furthermore, he was 23 years old at this point, not 28.
	Zhang Fei did not whip the imperial inspector. Liu Bei did.
	Was not offered Xuzhou by Tao Qian directly, but was invited to govern by Chen Deng and Kong Rong. He also never offered Xuzhou to Lü Bu.
	Was not acknowledged by Emperor Xian as Imperial Uncle.
	Did not rely on Xu Shu to defend Xinye from Cao Ren and Li Dian. He arranged and carried out the defense himself.
	Defeated Xiahou Dun at Xin Ye, not Zhuge Liang.
	Though involved in Chibi, Wu’s role was greater in history. View ≈5 Additional Facts?
	Although Sun Quan was advised by Zhou Yu and Han Ze to keep Liu Bei hostage during his visit to Wu, no such attempt was made, and Liu Bei left without incident.
	Huang Zhong was not chief commander of Shu’s Hanzhong campaign. Liu Bei was.
	Commanded between 40–80,000 troops at Yiling; far fewer than the 700,000 or so mentioned in the novel.
	Had more than three sons and some daughters (which were, unfortunately, captured by Cao Chun at Changban Slope).
	Historically Liu Bei was a much more talented commander than the novel makes him out to be.

sections.short-biography: A major warlord who claimed heritage from the Han. He founded Shu-Han.
sections.details:
Traditional Chinese 
	劉備 (玄德)


	 Simplified Chinese 
	刘备 (玄德)


	 Pronunciation 
	Liu2 Bei4 (Xuan2de2) 


	 Pinyin (Tones) 
	Liú Bèi (Xuándé)


	 Wade-Giles 
	Liu Pei (Hsüan-tê)

	 Birthplace 
	Zhuo county, Zhuo commandery

	 Other Names 
	Imperial Uncle, Uncle Liu, Princely One, Xianzhu, First Ruler (先主), First Soverign

	 Name Notes 
	Liu Bei is commonly referenced by the name, Xianzhu (先主), ‘First Ruler’ or ‘First Soverign’ [of Shu] (蜀先主). Chen Shou, in the Sanguozhi, refers to Liu Bei by this title to indicate the illegitimacy of his dynasty in the eyes of Jin. Sun Quan and his clan are placed on an even lower pedestal. Sometimes referred to as the ‘Princely One’ in the Brewitt-Taylor novel translation.

sections.popular-quotations:
Liu Bei said, “However, you may return and tell Sun Quan to wash his neck: the executioner is coming!”[且放汝回去，說與孫權，洗頸就戮！]
			Romance of the Three Kingdoms: Chapter 82
		
	
				
		
			In his testament to Liu Shan, Liu Bei said, “Be vigilant! Be vigilant! If an evil is minor, resist it nonetheless. If a good deed is trifling, perform it all the same. Only wisdom and virtue can truly win men’s devotion.”
			Romance of the Three Kingdoms: Chapter 85

sections.biography-source-undefined:
Liu Bei, styled Xuande, was from Zhuo county, Zhuo commandery.

Liu Bei lacked a beard.[SGZ 42]

full_text:
Liu Bei (Xuande) 劉備 (玄德)
ARomance of the Three KingdomsEncyclopedia officer profile.
Liu Bei (劉備), styledXuande (玄德), was born in Zhuo county, Zhuo commandery. A major warlord who claimed heritage from the Han. He founded Shu-Han. Liu Bei was affiliated with the Han Dynasty, Gongsun Zan, Tao Qian, the Wei Kingdom, Yuan Shao, Liu Biao, and the Shu Kingdom. Liu Bei lived during the Later Han Dynasty and Three Kingdoms periods of Chinese history.
Return to theThree Kingdoms Encyclopediaor browse theEncyclopedia Directory.
Return toThree Kingdoms Encyclopedia

Short Biography
A major warlord who claimed heritage from the Han. He founded Shu-Han.
Details
Traditional Chinese劉備 (玄德)
Simplified Chinese刘备 (玄德)
PronunciationLiu2Bei4(Xuan2de2)
Pinyin (Tones)Liú Bèi (Xuándé)
Wade-GilesLiu Pei (Hsüan-tê)
BirthplaceZhuo county, Zhuo commandery
Other NamesImperial Uncle,Uncle Liu,Princely One,Xianzhu,First Ruler (先主),First Soverign
Name NotesLiu Bei is commonly referenced by the name, Xianzhu (先主), ‘First Ruler’ or ‘First Soverign’ [of Shu] (蜀先主). Chen Shou, in theSanguozhi, refers to Liu Bei by this title to indicate the illegitimacy of his dynasty in the eyes of Jin. Sun Quan and his clan are placed on an even lower pedestal. Sometimes referred to as the ‘Princely One’ in the Brewitt-Taylor novel translation.

Fact vs Fiction
Differences Between Fact and Common Fiction
The Peach Tree Garden oath did not actually take place butSanguozhistates that Liu, Guan and Zhang ‘slept in the same bed and wereas close as brothers.’
Played a minor role during the Yellow Scarves revolt. Did not fight the Scarves at Wan or save Dong Zhuo. Furthermore, he was 23 years old at this point, not 28.
Zhang Fei did not whip the imperial inspector. Liu Bei did.
Was not offered Xuzhou by Tao Qian directly, but was invited to govern by Chen Deng and Kong Rong. He also never offered Xuzhou to Lü Bu.
Was not acknowledged by Emperor Xian as Imperial Uncle.
Did not rely on Xu Shu to defend Xinye from Cao Ren and Li Dian. He arranged and carried out the defense himself.
Defeated Xiahou Dun at Xin Ye, not Zhuge Liang.
Though involved in Chibi, Wu’s role was greater in history.
View ≈5 Additional Facts?
Although Sun Quan was advised by Zhou Yu and Han Ze to keep Liu Bei hostage during his visit to Wu, no such attempt was made, and Liu Bei left without incident.
Huang Zhong was not chief commander of Shu’s Hanzhong campaign. Liu Bei was.
Commanded between 40–80,000 troops at Yiling; far fewer than the 700,000 or so mentioned in the novel.
Had more than three sons and some daughters (which were, unfortunately, captured by Cao Chun at Changban Slope).
Historically Liu Bei was a much more talented commander than the novel makes him out to be.
Literary Appearances
Romance of the Three Kingdoms:1–2,5–7,11–28,31–45,47–49,51–58,60–74,76–84,85,86–89,91,93,97,102–106,117–120
Sanguozhi:Shu 2, Shu 11, Wei 6
Popular Quotations
Liu Bei said,“However, you may return and tell Sun Quan to wash his neck: the executioner is coming!”
[且放汝回去，說與孫權，洗頸就戮！]
Romance of the Three Kingdoms: Chapter 82
In his testament to Liu Shan, Liu Bei said,“Be vigilant! Be vigilant! If an evil is minor, resist it nonetheless. If a good deed is trifling, perform it all the same. Only wisdom and virtue can truly win men’s devotion.”
Romance of the Three Kingdoms: Chapter 85
